coredata.dc:title: Effective and selective measurement of 2,4-dinitroaniline utilizing constructed fluorescent carbon dots sensor derived from vitamin B1
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuangying
Zhou, Qingxiang
Li, Zhi
Liu, Menghua
Li, Yanhui
Chen, Chunmao
coredata.dc:description: 2,4-Dinitroaniline is a multifunctional product and has been applicated in various fields. It has absorbed much concerns due to its large consumption and occurrence in many environmental matrices. It is urgent to explore reliable and sensitive measurement technology. Present study described an outstanding fluorescence sensor constructed with carbon dots (CDs) derived from vitamin B1. The CDs were fabricated by dealing with a facile hydrothermal process, and exhibited good water solubility, fluorescence stability and highly fluorescence quenching characteristics in presence of 2,4-dinitroaniline. The excitation and emission wavelengths of the obtained CDs were 305 and 378 nm, respectively. The sensor displayed good selectivity and sensitivity for 2,4-dinitroaniline. Good linearity can be achieved in two-stage of 0.2–3 μmol L−1 and 3–20 μmol L−1. The sensor earned low detection limit for 2,4-dinitroaniline down to 0.05 μmol L−1. The sensor has been commendably employed for analysis of 2,4-dinitroaniline in real aqueous samples with spiked recoveries in the range of 95.0–99.6%. The proposed sensor provided a promising alternative for analysis of environmental pollutants or other meaningful employment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
2,4-Dinitroaniline
Vitamin B1
Hydrothermal method

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthesis and detection principle of the proposed fluorescent sensor.

Characterization of the prepared CDs. (a) TEM image and size histogram of CDs. (b) 3D contour photo of the prepared CDs; (c) Fluorescent spectra at various excitation wavelengths; (d) UV–vis spectrum of 2,4-dinitroaniline (5 μmol L−1), prepared CDs (20 μg mL−1) and emission spectrum of the prepared CDs; (e) XPS spectra of O1s, N1s, S2p and C1s of the prepared CDs.

(A) Impact of pH on the fluorescent intensity of the prepared CDs; (B) Stability of the prepared CDs; (C) Impact of CDs concentration on ΔF; (D) Impact of pH value on detection. (E) Impact of ionic strength on ΔF; (F) Impact of incubation time.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, 2,4-dinitroaniline and CDs − 2,4-dinitroaniline mixture (B) Zeta potentials o of CDs and CDs − 2,4-dinitroaniline mixture.

Fluorescence intensities of CDs in the presence of different concentration of 2,4-dinitroaniline and linear correlation of ΔF values against 2,4-dinitroaniline concentration within the range of 0.2–20 μM.
Investigation of selectivity of fluorescence CDs for sensing 2,4-dinitroaniline.
The spiked recoveries of real samples at three levels with developed sensor.
Comparison of different fluorescent sensors for the detection of 2,4-dinitroaniline.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: MOF: Metal–organic framework; DMF: N, N-Dimethylformamide; Ln@[Zn(L)2] (CH3OH)0.5⋅(H2O)2 :(Ln = lanthanides,Tb/ Eu; L = 3-chloro-6-(3-methyl-5-phenyl-1H-pyrazol-1-yl) picolinic acid).

head.ce:title: Effective and selective measurement of 2,4-dinitroaniline utilizing constructed fluorescent carbon dots sensor derived from vitamin B1

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum-Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 2,4-Dinitroaniline is a multifunctional product and has been applicated in various fields. It has absorbed much concerns due to its large consumption and occurrence in many environmental matrices. It is urgent to explore reliable and sensitive measurement technology. Present study described an outstanding fluorescence sensor constructed with carbon dots (CDs) derived from vitamin B1. The CDs were fabricated by dealing with a facile hydrothermal process, and exhibited good water solubility, fluorescence stability and highly fluorescence quenching characteristics in presence of 2,4-dinitroaniline. The excitation and emission wavelengths of the obtained CDs were 305 and 378 nm, respectively. The sensor displayed good selectivity and sensitivity for 2,4-dinitroaniline. Good linearity can be achieved in two-stage of 0.2–3 μmol L−1 and 3–20 μmol L−1. The sensor earned low detection limit for 2,4-dinitroaniline down to 0.05 μmol L−1. The sensor has been commendably employed for analysis of 2,4-dinitroaniline in real aqueous samples with spiked recoveries in the range of 95.0–99.6%. The proposed sensor provided a promising alternative for analysis of environmental pollutants or other meaningful employment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With rapid development of industrialization, the problem of environmental pollution is becoming more and more prominent [1]. Among thousands of different pollutants, toxic aromatic amines take great position. As one important kind of nitroaromatic compounds, 2,4-dinitroaniline is widely used in many fields including the synthesis of azo dyes, printing inks, preservatives, production of pesticides and other process of organic synthesis [2,3]. Due to its excellent chemical stability, 2,4-dinitroaniline discharged from various trades and professions exist in environment and is accumulated in ecosystem [4–6]. Though it is present in environment at trace level, its potential threat toward human body and global security could not be ignored. 2,4-dinitroaniline may lead to carcinogenic and genotoxic effects through skin, inhalation, ingestion because of its high toxicity and then enter respiratory system and gastrointestinal tract [7,8]. Thus, to establish efficient and convenient methods for detection of 2,4-dinitroaniline is of great significance and urgency.

The current methods for determination of nitroaromatics include electrochemical methods, liquid chromatography, liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry or gas chromatography-mass spectrometry, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, ion mobility spectroscopy and pulsed fast/-thermal neutron analysis [9–13], etc. Yet, most of the mentioned technologies not only rely on expensive instruments but also require long time for analysis [14,15]. Fluorescent method is an efficient and quick technique for measuring environment pollutants. Specially, the occurrence of many new nanomaterials owning fluorescence endows fluorescent method with more vitality and application prospect [16,17]. Among them, CDs gain great attention due to the unique characteristics of easy synthesis, higher fluorescence intensity, good water solubility, low toxicity, environment-friendliness and good biocompatibility [18,19]. Except employing for photocatalysis, bioimaging, drug delivery and optoelectronic devices, CDs also have been widely used as fluorescence sensor for sensing heavy metal ions and some important organic contaminants [20–25]. Peng’s group synthesized boron-nitrogen co-doped CDs (BNCDs) with dual-emission properties via hydrothermal method with 3-aminophenylboronic acid as carbon precursor, and presented a CDs and Cr (VI) system for determination of bisulfite in sugar. Cong et al. proposed fluorescence and electrochemiluminescence detection technology for measuring ferric ions based on polymer CDs (PCNDs). The PCNDs were achieved from l-tryptophan and l-phenylalanine by facile hydrothermal treatment. Li’s team established a sensitive and selective visual sensor for detecting benzoyl peroxide with CDs, in which CDs were prepared using hydrothermal treatment with p- and o- phenylenediamines, and dopamine as the precursors [26–28]. Synthesis methods of CDs can be divided into two typical ways that are “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods. Compared with“top-down” method, “bottom-up” method (hydro/solvothermal synthesis, microwave-assisted preparation, etc) has the advantages of lower cost and easy operation, meanwhile, the surface state and elemental composition of as-obtained CDs can be regulated by adding different precursors or changing reaction temperature, time and solvents during the synthetic process [29–32]. Vitamin B1 is a good precursor of CDs duo to its rich element composition and it can be easily obtained without high cost.

In this study, vitamin B1 was chosen as the precursor for synthesis of CDs by one step hydrothermal treatment. The fabricated CDs were characterized with specific instruments. The fluorescent CDs exhibited highly fluorescence quenching characteristics in presence of 2,4-dinitroaniline. An effective fluorescent sensor was successfully constructed for measuring 2,4-dinitroaniline in actual water samples, and the related experimental parameters were explored one by one, and Schematic diagram of the proposed fluorescent sensor was exhibited in Fig. 1
.

Vitamin B1, 2,4-dinitroaniline, aniline, 4-butylaniline, 2-nitroaniline and p-toluidine were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All other used reagents and the instruments for characterization and analysis were as the same in our heretofore works [31,33].

CDs were synthesized by facile hydrothermal treatment. The detailed information was as follows. Firstly, 0.5 g vitamin B1 was dispersed into 10 mL ultrapure water with the help of ultrasonication. Then the mixed solution was moved to 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept heating at 180 °C for 10 h, and which was cooled to ambient temperature, light yellow CD solution was obtained. Finally, the purification of CDs was completed by the aid of dialysis bag with molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da, and the purified CDs were obtained and stored.

In the analytical procedure, 20 μL CD solution (1 mg mL−1) was diluted with ultra-pure water to 1.0 mL in a quartz cuvette and the fluorescence intensity was recorded. Similarly, fluorescence intensity of 1 mL mixture containing 20 μL CD solution (1 mg mL−1) and a certain amount of 2,4-dinitroaniline was also recorded. The change of fluorescence intensity is the key to quantitative analysis of 2,4-dinitroaniline. The excitation and emission wavelengths were set at 305 nm and 378 nm, while slit widths were all set at 5 nm.

Three kinds of aqueous samples were collected from Binhe Park (Changping, Beijing, China), Jingmi Diversion Canal (Changping, Beijing, China), Xuanwu Lake (Nanjing, China), and then filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membrane and kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

As illustrated in Fig. 2
A, the as-obtained CDs were subspherical and their particle sizes were spread over 1.2–2.6 nm, and most of CDs was about 2 nm. Fig. 2 B and 2C exhibited fluorescent spectra of the prepared CDs. The emission wavelength occurred red shift and then blue shift along with the increase of excitation wavelength from 280 to 315 nm, which demonstrated that the synthesized CDs were excitation-dependent. Fluorescent signal of CDs raised with the increase of excitation wavelength from 280 to 305 nm and then declined with the continuous increase of excitation wavelength. The highest fluorescence intensity was obtained with excitation and emission wavelengths at 305 nm and 378 nm, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. 2 D, the weak UV–vis absorption around 245 nm was contributed to π-π* transition, suggesting a representative absorption of aromatic π system. Fig. 2 E displays XPS spectra of O1s, N1s, S2p and C1s of the as-obtained CDs. High resolution spectrum of O1s suggests the presence of C-OH (531.6 eV) bonds and N1s spectrum accounts for C–N bonds (399.4 eV). In addition, high-resolution spectra of S2p and C1s exhibited peaks of C-S-C (163.4 eV), C-SOX (166.3 eV) bonds and C=C (284.4 eV), C–O (286.5 eV) bonds, indicating the oxidative polymerization process of carbon source in CDs synthesis.

The preliminary experiments demonstrated that 2,4-dinitroaniline would significantly quench the fluorescent intensity of the prepared CDs. Based on this, CDs derived from vitamin B1 can be designed as an efficient sensing sensor for measuring 2,4-dinitroaniline. Factors that affect the change value (F0–F, ΔF) of the fluorescent intensity from CDs were optimized. In addition, the impact of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the obtained CDs and fluorescence stability of CDs within one month were also investigated. As exhibited in Fig. 3
A, the fluorescent response of CDs increased slightly with the rise of pH in the range of 2–6, and then decreased obviously when the solution pH rose from 6 to 8. Similarly, the effect of pH on ΔF was consistent with its effect on CDs. Fig. 3 D shows that the ΔF value raised gradually with sample pH increasing from 2 to 6, and then decreased with continuous increase of the pH value. Stability of the prepared CDs was investigated by measuring the fluorescent intensity of the obtained CDs under the same condition at different time. As shown in Fig. 3 B, the obtained CDs exhibited good stability within one month. CDs concentration is also another significant factor influencing the ΔF value. Fig. 3 C shows that ΔF value raised with the concentration increase of CDs from 5 to 20 mg L−1 and then decreased with continuous concentration increase of CDs. Thus, the CDs concentration was fixed at 20 mg L−1 for determination of 2,4-dinitroaniline in further study. In order to observe the impact of ionic strength on ΔF value, NaCl was added into sample solution in the range of 0–20% (w/v) in the procedure of fluorescence detection of 2,4-dinitroaniline. Evidently, the addition of NaCl will negatively influence the detection of 2,4-dinitroaniline (See Fig. 3 E). Therefore, no NaCl was fortified in subsequent study. Fig. 3 F exhibits that the interaction of CDs and 2,4-dinitroaniline was quite rapid and could maintain stable at least 60 min.

There are many applications of CDs for detecting various analytes based on the principle that fluorescence intensity of CDs can be weakened or enhanced in the presence of analytes, which can be contributed to the fact that interaction of CDs and target analytes will lead to quenching or suppressing quenching of the fluorescent intensity of as-prepared CDs. Generally, quenching mechanisms involves static quenching, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), energy transfer, dynamic quenching and inner filter effect (IFE), etc. Among energy transfer mechanisms, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), Dexter energy transfer (DET) and Surface energy transfer (SET) mechanisms are included. According to Fig. 2 D, the emission spectrum of the as-prepared CDs and the absorption spectrum of 2,4-dinitroaniline were partly overlapped, and so the quenching mechanism may be IFE. For furtherly exploring the sensing mechanism, UV–vis spectra of CDs, 2,4-dinitroaniline and CDs − 2,4-dinitroaniline mixture were explored. As seen in Fig. 4
A, there’ re no new absorption peak appear in mixture of CDs and 2,4-dinitroaniline, indicating that no complex formed. And the zeta potential of the as-prepared CDs just declined from −1.96 mV to −2.37 mV companied with the addition of 2,4-dinitroaniline (See Fig. 4 B). This may be contributed to the mechanism of IFE.

As can be seen from Fig. 5 , the fluorescent intensity of the as-prepared CDs decreased progressively with the increasing addition of 2,4-dinitroaniline. Good linear ranges could be obtained when 2,4-dinitroaniline concentration was in the two-stage of 0.2–3 μM and 3–20 μM. In addition, the limit of detection for 2,4-dinitroaniline was 0.02 μM according to 3σ/s, where σ represented standard deviation of the blank signals (n = 15) and s represented the slope of the calibration curve in the low concentration range. Compared with reported literatures about fluorescent sensors for the detection of 2,4-dinitroaniline, preparation of proposed sensor was more easily and less time was needed. Moreover, time for analysis was within 1 min less than other reported works [34,35].

For the purpose of assessing the selectivity of the developed CDs based fluorescence sensor for 2,4-dinitroaniline determination, five times higher concentration of other aniline analogs were fortified into sample spiked with 2,4-dinitroaniline (2 μM). It can be seen from Fig. 6 that there are no massively change in ΔF value, indicating good selectivity of proposed CDs for sensing 2,4-dinitroaniline. The results proved the good sensing performance of proposed CDs toward 2,4-dinitroaniline detection.

For estimating the applicability of the proposed sensor for 2,4-dinitroaniline determination, three real water samples taken from Binhe Park, Jingmi Diversion Canal and Xuanwu Lake and spiked real water samples at 1, 2 and 5 μM were analyzed. As exhibited in Table 1
, the experimental results presented that the spiked recoveries of 2,4-dinitroaniline in the range of 95.0%-99.6% (n = 3) with RSDs between 1.3% and 3.9%. The results proved that this constructed fluorescent sensor for 2,4-dinitroaniline was reliable and sensitive.

In this study, CDs derived from vitamin B1 were fabricated using hydrothermal treatment and employed as a sensitive fluorescent sensor for sensing 2,4-dinitroaniline. In the presence of 2,4-dinitroaniline, CDs-2,4-dinitroaniline complex may be formed, triggering the aggregation of CDs and causing obvious fluorescence quenching of CDs. Based on the obvious fluorescence change of CDs. The sensor provided good linear range in two-stage of 0.2–3 μM and 3–20 μM. Moreover, the sensor possessed high anti-interference, exhibiting good selectivity for analysis of 2,4-dinitroaniline. In addition, compared with other reported different fluorescent sensors toward 2,4-dinitroaniline, the proposed sensor was more convenient and faster. All the results demonstrated that the proposed sensor was an efficient and simple method for detection of 2,4-dinitroaniline and which also exhibited the outstanding application potential. The prepared CDs fluorescence sensor was selective for determination of 2,4-dinitroaniline, however it was also a drawback for multi-pollutants analysis in various fields. There are few applications for simultaneous detection of multiple pollutants with CDs based sensors. To explore stable and specific CDs for such purposes may be a promising direction, and which may promote the progress of CDs development and application.

Shuangying Li: Project administration, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Qingxiang Zhou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Visualization. Zhi Li: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Menghua Liu: Investigation, Software. Yanhui Li: Data curation, Formal analysis. Chunmao Chen: Investigation, Software, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

Appl. Spectrosc.
